title: Bacon and egg sandwich
subtitle: Crisp back bacon and a fried egg in soft white bread
excerpt: Bacon and egg sandwich is what happens when you want more than a bacon sandwich. The egg goes in alongside the bacon, the yolk breaks when you bite down, and everything comes together in the bread. This is the British cafe classic done at home.
sourceNotes: Standard British breakfast combination found in every cafe. The bacon-and-egg sandwich has been a feature of British cafes and transport cafes since at least the 1950s, and remains one of the most ordered breakfast items in the country.

content.text: The bacon-and-egg sandwich requires back bacon cooked until the fat is crisp, and a fried egg with a runny yolk. The egg sits on top of the bacon in the bread, and the yolk runs when you press down. Use soft white bread, butter both pieces, and choose your condiment. Do not rush the bacon.

items.prepNote: divided, for the pan and the bread

content.text: Heat a large frying pan over a medium-high heat. Add the bacon rashers and cook for 2 to 3 minutes per side until the fat is crisp. Push the bacon to one side of the pan.
Add a knob of butter to the empty side of the pan. Once it foams, crack in the eggs and fry for 2 to 3 minutes until the white is set and the yolk is still runny.
Butter the bread. Place two rashers on the base of each sandwich, then add an egg on top. Add condiment if using, top with the second slice of bread, and serve at once.

items.symptom: The yolk breaks in the pan
items.cause: Cracked into the pan from too high up, or the pan was too hot.
items.fix: Crack the egg into a small cup first, then slide it gently into the pan.

items.symptom: The bacon and egg are not ready at the same time
items.fix: Cook the bacon through first, push it to one side, then start the egg. Both finish within a minute of each other.

items.symptom: The egg white spreads too wide
items.cause: Pan not hot enough when the egg was added.
items.fix: Make sure the butter is foaming before adding the egg. This sets the white quickly and contains the spread.

content.text: The bacon-and-egg sandwich is the backbone of the British transport cafe and the template for the full English in a bun. It has been eaten at building sites and lorry parks since the 1950s, and the format has never changed. The egg yolk that runs into the bread when you bite is half the point of ordering it.
